commands.name: ps
commands.description: Report a snapshot of current processes

patterns.text: list all running processes with full details
patterns.explanation: Shows all processes for all users with CPU/memory usage

patterns.text: find a process by name
patterns.explanation: Filters process list for entries matching nginx

patterns.text: show processes in a tree hierarchy
patterns.explanation: Displays processes in a forest/tree format showing parent-child relationships

patterns.text: list processes sorted by memory usage
patterns.explanation: Shows top 20 processes consuming the most memory

patterns.text: show only the process IDs for a specific command
patterns.explanation: Lists PIDs of all sshd processes without headers

commands.name: top
commands.description: Display real-time system resource usage and running processes

patterns.text: monitor system processes in real time
patterns.explanation: Opens interactive process viewer showing CPU, memory, and process info

patterns.text: show processes for a specific user
patterns.explanation: Filters the top display to only show processes owned by www-data

patterns.text: run top in batch mode for scripting
patterns.explanation: Runs one iteration of top in non-interactive mode and shows top 20 lines

patterns.text: sort processes by memory consumption interactively
patterns.explanation: Starts top sorted by memory usage percentage

patterns.text: monitor a specific process by its PID
patterns.explanation: Tracks only the process with PID 1234

commands.name: htop
commands.description: Interactive process viewer with a colorful and user-friendly interface

patterns.text: open an interactive process manager
patterns.explanation: Launches the htop process viewer with color-coded resource bars

patterns.text: view processes belonging to a particular user
patterns.explanation: Filters htop to show only processes owned by the deploy user

patterns.text: show processes in a tree view
patterns.explanation: Opens htop with tree mode enabled to show parent-child relationships

patterns.text: monitor a specific process by PID in htop
patterns.explanation: Opens htop focused on the process with PID 5678

patterns.text: sort htop output by memory usage from the command line
patterns.explanation: Starts htop pre-sorted by memory usage percentage

commands.name: kill
commands.description: Send a signal to a process, typically to terminate it

patterns.text: terminate a process gracefully by its PID
patterns.explanation: Sends SIGTERM (default) to process 1234, allowing it to clean up

patterns.text: force kill an unresponsive process
patterns.explanation: Sends SIGKILL to immediately terminate process 1234 without cleanup

patterns.text: send a hangup signal to reload a daemon's configuration
patterns.explanation: Sends SIGHUP which many daemons interpret as a config reload request

patterns.text: list all available signal names
patterns.explanation: Prints all signal names that can be sent with kill

patterns.text: stop a process temporarily
patterns.explanation: Suspends process 1234 so it stops executing but remains in memory

commands.name: killall
commands.description: Kill processes by name rather than PID

patterns.text: terminate all processes with a given name
patterns.explanation: Sends SIGTERM to every process named firefox

patterns.text: force kill all instances of a program
patterns.explanation: Sends SIGKILL to every process named python3

patterns.text: kill processes owned by a specific user
patterns.explanation: Terminates all nginx processes belonging to user john

patterns.text: ask for confirmation before killing each process
patterns.explanation: Interactively prompts before sending the signal to each matching node process

patterns.text: wait for all killed processes to die
patterns.explanation: Sends SIGTERM and waits until all apache2 processes have actually terminated

commands.name: pkill
commands.description: Send a signal to processes matching a pattern

patterns.text: kill processes whose names match a pattern
patterns.explanation: Terminates processes whose full command line matches the pattern

patterns.text: send a signal to all processes owned by a user
patterns.explanation: Sends SIGTERM to every process running under user bob

patterns.text: kill the newest instance of a process
patterns.explanation: Sends SIGTERM only to the most recently started chrome process

patterns.text: kill the oldest instance of a process
patterns.explanation: Sends SIGTERM only to the longest-running worker process

patterns.text: reload a service by sending SIGHUP to matching processes
patterns.explanation: Sends SIGHUP to syslogd processes to trigger a configuration reload

commands.name: pgrep
commands.description: Look up processes based on name or other attributes and return their PIDs

patterns.text: find the PID of a running program
patterns.explanation: Returns the PIDs of all processes named nginx

patterns.text: list processes matching a pattern with their names
patterns.explanation: Shows PIDs and full command lines of all processes matching python

patterns.text: count how many instances of a process are running
patterns.explanation: Returns the count of running processes named node

patterns.text: find processes owned by a specific user matching a pattern
patterns.explanation: Lists PIDs of sshd processes owned by root

patterns.text: find a process by its full command line
patterns.explanation: Matches against the full command line rather than just the process name

commands.name: nice
commands.description: Run a command with a modified scheduling priority

patterns.text: run a CPU-intensive task with lower priority
patterns.explanation: Runs make with the lowest priority so it does not hog the CPU

patterns.text: run a command with higher than normal priority
patterns.explanation: Runs the task with elevated priority (requires root)

patterns.text: start a background compression job at low priority
patterns.explanation: Compresses a file in the background without impacting other work

patterns.text: check the default nice value
patterns.explanation: Prints the current default niceness value (usually 0)

commands.name: renice
commands.description: Alter the scheduling priority of a running process

patterns.text: lower the priority of a running process
patterns.explanation: Sets the nice value of process 1234 to 10, making it lower priority

patterns.text: increase the priority of all processes for a user
patterns.explanation: Boosts priority for all processes owned by www-data

patterns.text: set all processes in a process group to low priority
patterns.explanation: Changes the niceness of all processes in process group 5000

patterns.text: make a background job nicer so it does not slow down interactive work
patterns.explanation: Gives the backup script the lowest possible priority

commands.name: nohup
commands.description: Run a command immune to hangups, with output redirected to a file

patterns.text: run a long task that continues after logging out
patterns.explanation: Starts the script in the background and it survives terminal close

patterns.text: run a command with nohup and redirect output to a custom file
patterns.explanation: Runs the training script immune to hangups with all output going to training.log

patterns.text: start a web server that persists after disconnecting from SSH
patterns.explanation: Keeps the Node server running even after the SSH session ends

patterns.text: run nohup and discard all output
patterns.explanation: Runs the script silently in the background with no output file

commands.name: bg
commands.description: Resume a suspended job in the background

patterns.text: resume the most recently suspended job in the background
patterns.explanation: Continues the last stopped job as a background process

patterns.text: resume a specific job number in the background
patterns.explanation: Resumes job number 2 to run in the background

patterns.text: suspend a running foreground process then background it
patterns.explanation: After pressing Ctrl-Z to suspend, sends job 1 to background

patterns.text: put a stopped download back into the background
patterns.explanation: Resumes the wget job to continue downloading in the background

commands.name: fg
commands.description: Bring a background job to the foreground

patterns.text: bring the most recent background job to the foreground
patterns.explanation: Moves the current background job to the foreground terminal

patterns.text: bring a specific background job to the foreground
patterns.explanation: Brings job number 2 to the foreground

patterns.text: resume a stopped vim session in the foreground
patterns.explanation: Returns to the suspended vim editor session

patterns.text: bring a named job back to the foreground
patterns.explanation: Brings the background make job back to the foreground

commands.name: jobs
commands.description: List active jobs in the current shell session

patterns.text: show all background and suspended jobs
patterns.explanation: Lists all jobs with their status (running, stopped, etc.)

patterns.text: list jobs with their process IDs
patterns.explanation: Shows jobs with their PIDs alongside the job numbers

patterns.text: show only running jobs
patterns.explanation: Filters the job list to only display currently running jobs

patterns.text: show only stopped jobs
patterns.explanation: Filters the job list to only display suspended/stopped jobs

commands.name: wait
commands.description: Wait for background processes to complete before continuing

patterns.text: wait for all background jobs to finish
patterns.explanation: Blocks until every background job in the current shell completes

patterns.text: wait for a specific background process to complete
patterns.explanation: Waits for the most recently launched background process to finish

patterns.text: run two tasks in parallel and wait for both to finish
patterns.explanation: Launches both tasks simultaneously and waits until both complete

patterns.text: wait for a specific PID and check its exit status
patterns.explanation: Waits for process 5678 and then prints its exit code

commands.name: disown
commands.description: Remove a job from the shell's job table so it is not killed on logout

patterns.text: detach a running background job from the shell
patterns.explanation: Removes job 1 from the job table so it survives shell exit

patterns.text: disown all background jobs at once
patterns.explanation: Removes all jobs from the shell job table

patterns.text: start a long process and then detach it from the terminal
patterns.explanation: Runs the task in background and immediately disowns it

patterns.text: prevent a job from receiving SIGHUP without removing it from the job table
patterns.explanation: Marks job 1 so it will not receive SIGHUP when the shell exits

commands.name: screen
commands.description: Terminal multiplexer that allows persistent sessions

patterns.text: start a new named screen session
patterns.explanation: Creates a new screen session with the name mysession

patterns.text: reattach to an existing screen session
patterns.explanation: Reconnects to the detached screen session named mysession

patterns.text: list all active screen sessions
patterns.explanation: Shows all screen sessions and their states (attached/detached)

patterns.text: force reattach to a screen session that is still attached elsewhere
patterns.explanation: Detaches the session from other terminals and reattaches it here

patterns.text: run a command in a new detached screen session
patterns.explanation: Creates a detached session named backup running the backup script

commands.name: tmux
commands.description: Terminal multiplexer for managing multiple terminal sessions

patterns.text: start a new tmux session with a name
patterns.explanation: Creates a new tmux session named dev

patterns.text: attach to an existing tmux session
patterns.explanation: Reconnects to the tmux session named dev

patterns.text: list all tmux sessions
patterns.explanation: Shows all active tmux sessions with their names and window counts

patterns.text: kill a tmux session by name
patterns.explanation: Terminates the tmux session named dev and all its windows

patterns.text: split the current tmux pane horizontally
patterns.explanation: Divides the current pane into two side-by-side panes

patterns.text: send a command to a running tmux session from outside
patterns.explanation: Types and executes a command in the dev session remotely

commands.name: watch
commands.description: Execute a command repeatedly and display the output in real time

patterns.text: monitor a directory listing every 2 seconds
patterns.explanation: Refreshes the directory listing every 2 seconds on screen

patterns.text: watch disk usage change over time
patterns.explanation: Updates the disk usage display every 5 seconds

patterns.text: highlight differences between successive outputs
patterns.explanation: Shows memory usage and highlights what changed since last refresh

patterns.text: run a command repeatedly until it succeeds then stop
patterns.explanation: Keeps running until the output changes, indicating the service responded

patterns.text: monitor the number of connections to a port
patterns.explanation: Updates every second with the count of established TCP connections

commands.name: timeout
commands.description: Run a command with a time limit and kill it if it exceeds the limit

patterns.text: run a command with a 30-second time limit
patterns.explanation: Kills the script if it has not finished within 30 seconds

patterns.text: set a timeout with a force kill if the process does not stop
patterns.explanation: Sends SIGTERM after 60s and SIGKILL 10s later if still running

patterns.text: limit a network request to 5 seconds
patterns.explanation: Terminates the curl request if it takes longer than 5 seconds

patterns.text: run a command with a 2-minute timeout and preserve the exit status
patterns.explanation: Returns the command's exit code instead of the timeout exit code

commands.name: time
commands.description: Measure how long a command takes to execute

patterns.text: measure how long a command takes to run
patterns.explanation: Reports real, user, and system time after the build completes

patterns.text: benchmark a script execution time
patterns.explanation: Shows wall clock and CPU time used by the Python script

patterns.text: time a pipeline of commands
patterns.explanation: Measures the total time for the entire pipeline

patterns.text: get detailed resource usage information for a command
patterns.explanation: Shows verbose timing including memory usage and context switches

commands.name: strace
commands.description: Trace system calls and signals made by a process

patterns.text: trace all system calls made by a command
patterns.explanation: Shows every system call ls makes while listing /tmp

patterns.text: attach strace to a running process
patterns.explanation: Begins tracing system calls of the process with PID 1234

patterns.text: trace only file-related system calls
patterns.explanation: Filters output to show only open, stat, access, and similar file calls

patterns.text: trace a process and show timestamps for each call
patterns.explanation: Adds wall-clock timestamps to each system call trace line

patterns.text: trace network-related system calls only
patterns.explanation: Shows only socket, connect, bind, and other network system calls

patterns.text: save strace output to a file for later analysis
patterns.explanation: Writes all trace output including child processes to trace.log

commands.name: ltrace
commands.description: Trace library calls made by a process

patterns.text: trace library calls made by a program
patterns.explanation: Shows all shared library function calls made by the application

patterns.text: attach ltrace to a running process
patterns.explanation: Begins tracing library calls of process 1234

patterns.text: trace both library and system calls
patterns.explanation: Shows both library calls and system calls interleaved

patterns.text: count library call statistics instead of tracing each one
patterns.explanation: Summarizes library calls with counts and time spent in each

commands.name: lsof
commands.description: List open files and the processes that opened them

patterns.text: show all open files on the system
patterns.explanation: Lists every open file, socket, and pipe across all processes

patterns.text: find which process is using a specific port
patterns.explanation: Shows the process listening on or connected to port 8080

patterns.text: list all files opened by a specific process
patterns.explanation: Shows every file descriptor held by process 1234

patterns.text: find which processes have a specific file open
patterns.explanation: Lists all processes that currently have syslog open

patterns.text: show all network connections for a specific process
patterns.explanation: Lists network connections belonging to process 1234

patterns.text: list all open TCP connections
patterns.explanation: Shows all established TCP connections on the system

commands.name: fuser
commands.description: Identify processes using files or sockets

patterns.text: find which process is using a file
patterns.explanation: Shows the PID of any process that has the file open

patterns.text: find and kill all processes using a mount point
patterns.explanation: Kills every process accessing the USB mount so it can be unmounted

patterns.text: check which process is listening on a TCP port
patterns.explanation: Shows the PID of the process bound to TCP port 80

patterns.text: show verbose details about processes using a directory
patterns.explanation: Displays user, PID, access type, and command for each process

commands.name: pstree
commands.description: Display a tree of processes showing parent-child relationships

patterns.text: show the entire process tree
patterns.explanation: Displays all processes in a tree rooted at init/systemd

patterns.text: show the process tree with PIDs
patterns.explanation: Includes the PID of each process in parentheses

patterns.text: display process tree for a specific user
patterns.explanation: Shows only the process tree belonging to user alice

patterns.text: show the ancestry of a specific process
patterns.explanation: Displays the chain of parent processes leading to PID 1234

patterns.text: highlight a specific process in the tree
patterns.explanation: Draws the full tree but highlights the path to PID 1234

commands.name: crontab
commands.description: Schedule commands to run automatically at specified times

patterns.text: edit the current user's cron schedule
patterns.explanation: Opens the crontab file in the default editor for editing

patterns.text: list all scheduled cron jobs
patterns.explanation: Displays the current user's crontab entries

patterns.text: remove all cron jobs for the current user
patterns.explanation: Deletes the entire crontab for the current user

patterns.text: schedule a script to run every day at midnight
patterns.explanation: Sets up a single cron job that runs backup.sh at 00:00 daily

patterns.text: view cron jobs for a different user
patterns.explanation: Lists the crontab entries belonging to the www-data user

commands.name: at
commands.description: Schedule a one-time command to run at a specified time

patterns.text: schedule a command to run at a specific time today
patterns.explanation: Runs report.sh at 2:30 PM today

patterns.text: schedule a task to run tomorrow morning
patterns.explanation: Restarts the app service at 6 AM the following day

patterns.text: list all pending at jobs
patterns.explanation: Shows all scheduled one-time jobs and when they will run

patterns.text: remove a scheduled at job
patterns.explanation: Deletes the pending at job with ID 5

patterns.text: schedule a command to run 30 minutes from now
patterns.explanation: Sends a desktop notification 30 minutes from the current time

commands.name: batch
commands.description: Schedule a command to run when system load is low enough

patterns.text: queue a heavy job to run when the system is idle
patterns.explanation: Runs the script when system load average drops below the threshold

patterns.text: schedule a batch job with multiple commands
patterns.explanation: Queues multiple commands to execute when the system is not busy

patterns.text: run a resource-intensive compilation when the server is idle
patterns.explanation: Starts the full parallel build only when system load is low

patterns.text: check pending batch jobs
patterns.explanation: Lists all queued batch and at jobs waiting to execute

commands.name: systemd-run
commands.description: Run a command as a transient systemd service or scope unit

patterns.text: run a command as a transient systemd service
patterns.explanation: Creates a temporary systemd unit named mytask to run the script

patterns.text: run a command with resource limits using systemd
patterns.explanation: Limits the application to 512 MB of memory via a systemd scope

patterns.text: run a one-shot timer that executes after a delay
patterns.explanation: Schedules cleanup.sh to run once, one hour from now

patterns.text: run a command as a different user via systemd
patterns.explanation: Executes the worker script as the nobody user in a transient unit

patterns.text: create a transient systemd timer that runs periodically
patterns.explanation: Runs the health check every 15 minutes using a transient timer

commands.name: iotop
commands.description: Simple top-like I/O monitor

patterns.text: monitor disk I/O usage by processes in real time
patterns.explanation: Displays a continuously updated table of I/O utilization

patterns.text: show only processes that are currently doing I/O
patterns.explanation: Filters out idle processes to focus on active disk usage

commands.name: taskset
commands.description: Retrieve or set a process's CPU affinity

patterns.text: run a command on specific cpu cores
patterns.explanation: Restricts the execution of the command to CPU cores 0 and 1

patterns.text: change cpu affinity of a running process
patterns.explanation: Modifies an existing process to only run on cores 0 and 1

commands.name: ionice
commands.description: Get or set program I/O scheduling class and priority

patterns.text: run a command with lowest i/o priority to avoid slowing down the system
patterns.explanation: Executes the command in the 'idle' scheduling class (class 3)
